expectations.expectation: Review begins by establishing the scope and context of what artifact is being evaluated
expectations.justification: Opens with 'Artifact Under Review' section: 'A TypeScript WebhookWorkerPool class intended to process incoming webhook events at ~500 events/second in a Node.js service.'

expectations.expectation: Identified the race condition: multiple calls to processNext can interleave across await boundaries, bypassing the processing boolean guard
expectations.justification: Finding F1 provides a detailed explanation: 'a microtask scheduled between them (e.g., a resolved Promise callback from another part of the system calling enqueue) can see processing === false, enter processNext, shift an event, and begin processing it — then the original call also enters processNext and shifts another event.' Traces the exact interleaving across the non-atomic gap between 'this.processing = false' and 'this.processNext()'.

expectations.expectation: Identified the race condition as one of the most critical issues, explaining the potential impact (data corruption, lost events, or duplicate processing)
expectations.justification: F1 is the first Fatal Challenge (highest severity tier, positioned first). Impact stated: 'Duplicate event processing, corrupted results Map state, unpredictable behavior under load.'

expectations.expectation: Identified that the pool processes events sequentially (one at a time), making it unable to handle 500 events/second throughput
expectations.justification: Finding F2: 'Despite the name WorkerPool, this class processes exactly one event at a time... At the stated load of 500 events/second, the queue grows by ~490-498 events/second.'

expectations.expectation: Quantifies or explains the throughput gap: at 500 events/second with sequential async processing, the queue will grow unboundedly
expectations.justification: F2 provides explicit quantitative reasoning: 'each event requires a network round-trip (fetch to event.callbackUrl), which at typical webhook latencies of 100-500ms means the pool processes 2-10 events/second. At the stated load of 500 events/second, the queue grows by ~490-498 events/second.' Also states 'Unbounded queue growth (~500 events/second added, ~2-10 events/second drained)' and 'Eventual memory exhaustion and process crash.'

expectations.expectation: Identified the unbounded results Map as a memory leak that grows without limit
expectations.justification: Finding S1: 'The results Map stores every processed event result and is never pruned. At 500 events/second, this accumulates ~43 million entries per day... ~4-8 GB of memory per day.'

expectations.expectation: Identified missing retry logic for failed webhook deliveries
expectations.justification: Finding S2: 'Webhook delivery failures (network timeouts, 5xx responses from the callback URL, DNS failures) are silently recorded as failed with no retry attempt.' Quantifies impact at ~43,000 lost events per day at 0.1% failure rate.

expectations.expectation: Every major finding includes a proposed fix or mitigation, not just identification of the problem
expectations.justification: F1 includes a while-loop fix and mentions p-queue. F2 includes a concurrent worker pool implementation with configurable concurrency and throughput math. S1 includes TTL-based cleanup and delete-on-read options with code. S2 includes exponential backoff retry with code. S3 includes AbortSignal.timeout fix with code.

expectations.expectation: Challenges are specific and actionable — citing exact lines or logic paths, not vague concerns
expectations.justification: F1 cites the exact two non-atomic statements ('this.processing = false; this.processNext()') and provides a step-by-step interleaving scenario. F2 quotes the processing guard code. S1 calculates 43M entries/day and 4-8GB memory. All findings reference specific code constructs and logic paths.

expectations.expectation: Findings are organized by severity with the most critical issues first and an overall assessment with verdict
expectations.justification: Organized into Fatal Challenges (F1, F2), Significant Challenges (S1, S2, S3), Minor Observations (4 bullet points), and Overall Assessment with verdict 'Fundamentally flawed' and confidence level 'High'.

expectations.expectation: Did NOT focus on code style, naming conventions, or formatting as primary findings
expectations.justification: All Fatal and Significant findings are substantive engineering issues (race condition, throughput bottleneck, memory leak, retry logic, timeouts). Code style concerns (err.message typing, missing Content-Type header) are relegated to Minor Observations only.

eval_feedback: Exemplary report. All 11 expectations met with thorough, quantitative analysis and actionable fixes throughout.
